title: SAP → Distributed Systems Translation Map
description: The single highest-leverage resource for an SAP engineer preparing for FAANG. Every SAP concept mapped to its distributed systems equivalent with interview-ready framing. Covers 24 concept pairs across 7 domains.

description.value:
This translation map is the core bridge between your SAP experience and the language FAANG interviewers expect. Here is how to use it:

1. Learn the pairs. For each SAP concept, understand its distributed systems equivalent. You do not need to memorize every pair — focus on the 10-12 that are most relevant to your experience.

2. Practice the framing. Each pair includes a 30-second interview-ready answer. Practice saying these out loud. Record yourself. The goal is to sound natural, not rehearsed.

3. Translate on the fly. In an interview, when you start to say "IDoc" or "RFC", catch yourself and translate: "We used an event-driven messaging pattern with guaranteed delivery, similar to Kafka with exactly-once semantics." The interviewer should hear distributed systems language, not SAP jargon.

4. Use the decision trees. When you are unsure which concept maps to what, follow the decision trees. They are designed for quick reference during mock interview practice.

y="280">Goal: The interviewer hears distributed systems, not SAP</text></svg>

This domain covers how SAP stores, retrieves, and moves data — mapped to FAANG equivalents. These are the most frequently probed concepts in system design interviews because every distributed system must handle data.

IDoc / ALE → Event Sourcing + Change Data Capture. Application Link Enabling (ALE) with Intermediate Documents (IDocs) is SAP's native event-driven messaging system. It detects changes to business objects (purchase order created, material changed), packages them into a standardized document format, and distributes them to subscribing systems. This is exactly the Change Data Capture (CDC) pattern: detect changes at the source, serialize them, and propagate them downstream. The IDoc type (e.g., ORDERS05) corresponds to a Protobuf schema or Avro schema. The ALE distribution model is an event routing table. In FAANG, this same pattern is implemented via Debezium (CDC from databases) → Kafka → downstream consumers. Interview framing: "I worked with an event-driven messaging system for 15 years before Kafka was popular. SAP ALE is CDC with guaranteed delivery — it detects business object changes and serializes them into typed documents (IDocs) that downstream systems consume. The architecture patterns are identical: schema evolution, idempotent processing, exactly-once delivery."

Open SQL → ORM + Query Optimization. Open SQL abstracts over specific database syntax, providing a unified query interface. This is exactly an Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) layer: it handles connection pooling, statement caching, result set mapping, and database dialect differences. When FAANG asks about ORM performance (N+1 queries, lazy loading, select N+1), describe your experience tuning Open SQL: side effects of FOR ALL ENTRIES (N+1 scenario), why CDS with parameterized views avoids N+1, and how database hints in Open SQL map to query optimizer directives. Interview framing: "ABAP Open SQL is an ORM with 25 years of optimization. It handles connection pooling, prepared statement caching, and result set buffering. I tuned Open SQL queries that process millions of rows by understanding how the abstraction layer translates to HANA execution plans."

Internal Tables → In-Memory Data Structures. ABAP internal tables are typed, in-memory data structures with three access modes: STANDARD (array, sequential access), SORTED (sorted array, binary search), and HASHED (hash table, O(1) key lookup). This is a textbook implementation of fundamental data structures. FAANG coding interviews test exactly this: choosing the right data structure for the access pattern. When you practice LeetCode, your ABAP intuition for table type selection (hash vs sorted vs standard) directly transfers to choosing HashMap vs TreeMap vs ArrayList in Java or Python. Interview framing: "I choose data structures professionally every day. ABAP internal tables give me three runtime-optimized options: hash tables for O(1) key lookups, sorted tables for range scans, and standard tables for sequential access. The tradeoff is identical to choosing a HashMap vs TreeMap vs ArrayList."

SAP Client Concept → Multi-Tenant Data Isolation. The SAP client (mandt) is a tenant identifier that partitions data within a single database schema. Three isolation models exist: separate clients (independent databases — highest isolation, highest cost), client-dependent tables shared (shared data, some tenant-specific tables — hybrid), and cross-client data (truly shared tables with client filter). This is exactly the multi-tenancy problem FAANG asks about: separate databases per tenant vs shared database with tenant ID column vs hybrid. Interview framing: "I designed multi-tenant data isolation in SAP using the client concept. There are three isolation models: separate databases for each tenant (SAP client, highest isolation), shared with tenant-ID filtering (client-dependent tables), and hybrid. The tradeoffs are identical to designing a SaaS multi-tenant database on AWS."

rows: SAP couples schema to app server; FAANG decouples via schema registry
HIGH — asked in every system design about integration
SAP auto-handles connection pool; FAANG expects explicit pool tuning
MEDIUM — shows understanding of abstraction layers
SAP is schema-based; FAANG uses partition-based or DB-per-tenant
SAP CDS is HANA-aware; FAANG uses Redis/ElastiCache + RDBMS views
MEDIUM — shows data layer design maturity
HANA is OLTP+OLAP hybrid; most FAANG column stores are OLAP-only
MEDIUM — shows understanding of storage engine tradeoffs

quantifiedTradeoff.title: CDC vs Polling for Detecting Data Changes

variables.name: Change detection latency
variables.source: Debezium CDC benchmark; SAP ALE change pointer polling interval

variables.name: Infrastructure complexity
variables.value: CDC: High (log reader, schema registry, stream processing); Polling: Low (scheduled job, delta query)

variables.name: Data loss risk
variables.value: CDC: Near zero (transaction log source of truth); Polling: Moderate (gap between polls)

cp.monthlyPenalty: CDC path: event streaming with schema registry. Best for: sub-second latency, high volume, critical data. SAP analogue: ALE with change pointers (near-real-time).

ap.monthlyPenalty: Polling path: scheduled delta queries. Best for: moderate latency (seconds), low volume, non-critical data. SAP analogue: batch job reading CDS delta views.

calculation.breakeven: At 10K+ changes/day or 5-second latency requirement, CDC is cheaper than polling. Below that threshold, polling wins on simplicity.

description.value:
This domain covers how systems talk to each other — the most visible part of any architecture interview. SAP engineers have deep practical experience with enterprise integration patterns that map directly to modern API design.

RFC / BAPI → gRPC + Stateful RPC. Remote Function Call (RFC) is SAP's synchronous RPC protocol. BAPIs are typed service contracts defined in the Business Object Repository — exactly like gRPC services defined in Protobuf (.proto) files. RFC supports transactional RFC (tRFC, at-least-once delivery) and queued RFC (qRFC, ordered delivery), which map to gRPC's retry semantics and ordering guarantees. The key difference: RFC is stateful (connection tied to SAP logon session), while gRPC is stateless by design. Interview framing: "RFC is a synchronous RPC framework with typed contracts (BAPIs), connection pooling, and transactional semantics. It is gRPC for enterprise — the BAPI definition is a .proto file, and tRFC provides exactly-once delivery semantics."

SOAP → REST Maturity Model + GraphQL. SAP's extensive use of SOAP web services (in PI/PO, XI, and the old Web Service Framework) gives you direct experience with the most rigorous, schema-enforced RPC model ever built. The transition from SOAP to REST is a textbook lesson in the Richardson Maturity Model: Level 0 (SOAP, single URI, all operations via POST), Level 1 (individual resources), Level 2 (HTTP verbs), Level 3 (HATEOAS hypermedia). SAP engineers who have used both SOAP (in PI) and OData (in Gateway) have experienced this progression first-hand. Interview framing: "I have designed APIs at every level of the Richardson Maturity Model. In SAP, we progressed from SOAP (Level 0 — single endpoint, all POST) to OData (Level 2 — resources, verbs, query parameters) to RESTful APIs with hypermedia controls (Level 3). I understand the tradeoffs of each approach."

Event Mesh → Kafka / Pub-Sub. SAP Event Mesh is a managed message broker on BTP that supports AMQP 1.0 and MQTT. Under the hood, it uses RabbitMQ and Kafka. The concepts are identical: topics, partitions, consumer groups, offset management, retention policies, exactly-once delivery. When FAANG asks about Kafka, describe your Event Mesh experience — the principles are the same. Interview framing: "SAP Event Mesh is a managed Kafka-in-a-box. I designed event-driven architectures using topics, partitions, and consumer groups — the exact same concepts as Kafka, just with a simplified management layer. I understand Kafka's partition rebalancing, consumer group coordination, and retention-triggered compaction."

PI/PO / CPI → Integration Platform as a Service (iPaaS). SAP Process Integration / Process Orchestration and Cloud Platform Integration are enterprise integration platforms. They handle protocol translation (SOAP↔REST↔RFC), message transformation (XSLT, Groovy, JavaScript), routing (content-based, header-based), and monitoring. This is exactly an iPaaS — Workato, MuleSoft, Dell Boomi, or Apache Camel. When FAANG asks about integration platforms, describe your experience designing integration flows. Interview framing: "I designed integration flows on SAP CPI — an integration platform-as-a-service that handles protocol translation, message transformation, and content-based routing. The patterns I used (canonical data model, fan-out/scatter-gather, aggregator) are identical to designing flows on Apache Camel or MuleSoft."

decisionTree.title: Choosing Your Integration Pattern in an Interview
decisionTree.prompt: When the interviewer asks 'How would you design the communication between these two services?' — use this tree.
decisionTree.firstQuestion: Is the communication synchronous (wait for response) or asynchronous (fire-and-forget)?

branches.condition: Synchronous — must wait for response within SLA
branches.path: Describe gRPC (your RFC experience). Discuss connection pooling, timeout handling, retry with exponential backoff, circuit breaker. Reference: 'In SAP, RFC handles this with wp_no_rfc and tRFC for reliability. In FAANG, gRPC with interceptor chains achieves the same.'

Describe event-driven messaging (your Event Mesh / ALE experience). Discuss topics, partitions, consumer groups, exactly-once delivery. Reference: 'In SAP, ALE with IDocs distributes business object changes via event-driven messaging. In FAANG, Kafka with Avro schemas achieves the same with higher throughput.'

Describe CQRS pattern. Commands go through gRPC (synchronous, strong consistency). Events go through Kafka (asynchronous, eventual consistency). Reference: 'In SAP RAP, the managed scenario handles this via EML for commands and Event Mesh for side effects.'

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The best Staff+ answers discuss tradeoffs, not just choices. Always add: 'Given X volume, Y latency requirement, and Z consistency needs, I would choose X because...' Never present a single option without evaluation criteria.

sections.title: Transaction & Concurrency

description.value:
This is the domain where SAP engineers have the deepest advantages over FAANG candidates who only know modern frameworks. SAP's transaction management is battle-tested at planetary scale. The key is translating this experience into FAANG language.

SAP LUW → Saga Pattern + Compensating Transactions. The SAP Logical Unit of Work (LUW) is a transaction boundary that spans multiple database commits. An update in SAP is not finalized until all related updates are complete — if any step fails, the entire LUW is rolled back or compensated (via V3 update bundling, for example). This is exactly the Saga pattern from distributed systems: a sequence of local transactions where each step publishes an event or performs an action, and failures trigger compensating actions. The SAP travel management scenario (booking flight → hotel → car) is a textbook saga. Interview framing: "SAP LUW is the Saga pattern, invented 20 years before distributed systems textbooks. It manages multi-step business transactions where each step has a compensating action. In FAANG terms, I designed saga choreography with compensation handlers."

ABAP Enqueue → Lease-Based Optimistic Concurrency + ETCD. The ABAP enqueue mechanism (via function module ENQUEUE_*) provides application-level locking. Unlike database locks (which hold DB resources), enqueue locks are held in a shared memory table on the enqueue server. If the work process crashes, the lock is automatically released after a configurable timeout (the "rollback" time). This is a lease-based locking mechanism — exactly like ZooKeeper's ephemeral nodes or ETCD's lease-based locks. The key insight: SAP enqueue is a distributed lock manager. Interview framing: "The ABAP enqueue is a distributed lock manager with automatic lease expiration via timeouts. When a work process crashes, the enqueue server releases its locks after a configurable timeout — identical to how ETCD leases expire and ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes disappear."

V1/V2/V3 Update → Async Task Processing with Priorities. SAP update work processes handle post-commit business logic in three categories: V1 (critical, synchronous commitment — must complete before user gets response), V2 (non-critical, asynchronous — can be queued), and V3 (scheduled batch — deferred processing). This is an async task processing system with priority levels and delivery guarantees. In FAANG terms: V1 is a synchronous side effect (must complete), V2 is an SQS queue with at-least-once delivery, V3 is a scheduled batch job (Cron/Scheduler). Interview framing: "SAP update work processes implement async task processing with three priority tiers. V1 tasks are critical path — functionally identical to transactional outbox with immediate processing. V2 tasks are async queues with retry semantics. V3 tasks are batch/scheduled jobs."

Database LUW → ACID + 2PC. The database-level LUW in SAP is a standard ACID transaction with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK. SAP extends this with bundling (multiple DB LUWs grouped into one SAP LUW) and two-phase commit coordination across RFC destinations. When SAP updates multiple databases in a single transaction (via RFC, CPI, or EML + side effects), it uses a distributed transaction protocol functionally equivalent to X/Open XA (2PC). Interview framing: "SAP supports distributed transactions across systems via transactional RFC (tRFC) and CPI's exactly-once delivery. When a transaction spans multiple databases, SAP coordinates commit or rollback across all participants — functionally equivalent to two-phase commit."

sections.codeBlock:
" ABAP Enqueue → ETCD Lease Lock (conceptual equivalent)
" SAP ENQUEUE_* function modules
CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_E_TABLE'
  EXPORTING
    MODE_TABLE   = 'SFLIGHT'
    MANDT        = sy-mandt
    CARRID       = 'AA'
    CONNID       = '0017'
    _SCOPE       = '3'   " Lock scope: 1=local, 2=update, 3=all
    _WAIT        = 'X'   " Wait if locked
  EXCEPTIONS
    FOREIGN_LOCK = 1.

" Equivalent distributed system concept:
" ETCD lease-based lock with TTL
" etcdctl lock mylock --ttl 60
" If holder crashes → lease expires → lock auto-released
" SAP enqueue does the same: work process crash → enqueue server releases lock after timeout

adversarialResponse.title: Handling 'These transaction patterns sound outdated — why would FAANG use them?'
adversarialResponse.challenge: "The Saga pattern and distributed locking you described from SAP sound like legacy enterprise patterns. FAANG uses modern approaches. Why would we use SAP patterns today?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "SAP patterns are not legacy — they power the world's largest enterprises." (defensive — sounds like you are defending SAP, not demonstrating engineering judgment)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "These are not SAP patterns — they are distributed systems patterns that SAP implements. The Saga pattern was formalized in 1987 (by Garcia-Molina & Salem). SAP implemented it in the 1990s. FAANG re-implemented it in the 2010s. The algorithm is the same — only the infrastructure changes. When Uber needs a multi-step booking transaction (book car → calculate price → assign driver → notify user), they use a saga. When SAP needs a multi-step order-to-cash transaction (create order → check credit → confirm delivery), it uses a SAP LUW. The differences: (1) FAANG uses event-driven sagas with Kafka/Temporal, SAP uses database-driven LUWs; (2) FAANG compensates via explicit compensation events, SAP via implicit rollback; (3) FAANG has better observability (distributed tracing), SAP has debugger-level SM50. The fundamental architecture is identical: a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions on failure. I understand both implementations."
adversarialResponse.pattern: reframe-as-universal — SAP patterns are not legacy; they are time-tested implementations of universal distributed systems algorithms. Show you understand the algorithm (Saga, 2PC, lease-based locking) independently of the implementation (SAP LUW, Enqueue, tRFC).

description.value:
This domain reveals the biggest gap between SAP and FAANG engineering cultures. SAP deployment is release-based (transport every 2-4 weeks); FAANG is continuous (deploy 50-200 times per day). Understanding this gap and having a plan to bridge it is a strong Signal of adaptability.

SAP Transport System → Blue/Green Deployment + Canary Releases. The SAP transport system manages changes across landscape (DEV → QAS → PRD). A transport request captures changes, which are released together and migrated through the landscape in a coordinated way. This is conceptually closest to Blue/Green deployment: you have a current version (PRD) and a staging version (QAS), and you cut over by releasing the transport. However, FAANG deploys differently: Blue/Green with traffic switching, canary releases (2% → 5% → 25% → 100%), feature flags (gradual rollout with instant rollback), and A/B testing. Interview framing: "I understand the limitations of SAP's transport-based deployment. It is essentially a Blue/Green model without traffic splitting — you cut over all users at once. I am eager to work with feature flags, canary deployments, and gradual rollouts. The core concept (immutable deployment units promoted through environments) is the same; the speed and granularity are different."

gCTS → GitOps (ArgoCD / Flux). The gCTS (git-enabled Change and Transport System) connects SAP transport management to Git repositories. Changes are pushed to a Git branch, and gCTS pulls changes into the target system. This is exactly GitOps: Git as the single source of truth, pull-based deployment, declarative configuration. ArgoCD and Flux use the same pattern for Kubernetes deployments. Interview framing: "SAP gCTS implements GitOps for enterprise ABAP: Git as source of truth, pull-based synchronization, declarative transport definitions. The pattern is identical to ArgoCD — gCTS watches a Git branch and syncs changes to the target SAP system automatically."

ATC / Code Inspector → Static Analysis in CI (SonarQube / Semgrep). SAP ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) and Code Inspector are static code analysis tools. They check for performance issues, security vulnerabilities, functional defects, and compliance violations. In FAANG, the same function is served by SonarQube, Semgrep, ESLint, Checkstyle, and clang-tidy — running as CI pipeline gates. When FAANG fails a static analysis check, the PR is blocked. When ATC finds a critical issue, the transport is blocked. Interview framing: "SAP ATC is a static analysis platform that enforces code quality gates before deployment — the same function as SonarQube quality gates in a CI pipeline. I am familiar with the concept of automated code quality enforcement and understand how to extend it with custom checks (ATC variants → custom rules in Semgrep/CodeQL)."

SAP Short Dump → Crash / Core Dump Analysis. An SAP short dump (runtime error in ST22) is a crash dump — it captures the full program state at the moment of failure: call stack, variable values, memory address, database cursor position. FAANG engineers analyze core dumps (Java, C++, Go) with gdb, jstack, and heap dump analyzers. The diagnostic process is identical: read the stack trace, identify the failing method, examine variable state, reproduce in a lower environment. Interview framing: "I analyze production crash dumps regularly. SAP short dumps capture call stack, variable state, and memory context — the same information as a Java core dump or C++ segfault analysis. I use the same diagnostic process: read the stack trace, isolate the root cause, reproduce, and fix."

variables.name: Deployment frequency
variables.value: SAP transport: 1-2 / month; FAANG canary: 50-200 / day
variables.source: SAP best practices vs Google SRE book

variables.name: Rollback time
variables.value: SAP: hours (reverse transport + testing); FAANG canary: seconds (traffic shift back)

variables.name: Risk per deployment
variables.value: SAP: high (all users at once); FAANG canary: low (2% users first)

cp.monthlyPenalty: Transport model: large changes, monthly, full user exposure. Risk is high but mitigated by long testing cycles. Cost: 6-month regression testing phase before each major upgrade.

ap.monthlyPenalty: Canary model: small changes, daily, 2% exposure escalating. Risk is low but requires feature flags, monitoring, and automated rollback. Cost: complex infrastructure and engineering culture.

calculation.breakeven: At 50+ engineers deploying daily, canary model is cheaper. Below that, transport model is simpler operationally but riskier per deploy.

description.value:
SAP security is comprehensive but often misunderstood. The concepts translate well to FAANG authorization models.

SAP Authorization Objects → RBAC + ABAC. SAP's authorization concept is a textbook implementation of Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). Authorization objects (e.g., S_TCODE, S_CARRID) define permissions on specific resources. Authorization fields (ACTVT = activity: 01=create, 02=change, 03=display) define the operations allowed. This is identical to RBAC in AWS IAM (policies with actions on resources) or Azure RBAC (role definitions with permissions). The SAP concept of authorization objects with multiple fields (up to 10 fields per object) also extends into Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), where access decisions depend on contextual attributes (company code, plant, document type). Interview framing: "I designed an RBAC/ABAC system for 50,000 users. SAP authorization objects define permitted operations on resources, with multi-field conditions that evaluate at runtime. This is identical to designing AWS IAM policies with conditions or Azure RBAC with attribute-based rules."

SAP Security Concepts → OAuth2 + mTLS + Secrets Management. SAP's security infrastructure includes Secure Store (encrypted credential storage), SSF (Secure Store and Forward, for digital signatures and encryption), and the SAP Cryptographic Library. These map to: HashiCorp Vault (secrets management), OAuth2 token storage (encrypted credential storage), mTLS (mutual TLS for system-to-system authentication), and KMS (Key Management Service for encryption keys). Interview framing: "I implemented enterprise secrets management using SAP's Secure Store — an encrypted credential vault that applications access at runtime. The pattern is identical to HashiCorp Vault: credentials are stored encrypted at rest, accessed via APIs, and rotated periodically."

SAP Router / Firewall → Reverse Proxy + Zero Trust. SAProuter controls network access to SAP systems by defining which hosts and services can communicate (SAPRTR.DAT rules). This is an application-layer firewall with allow-list rules. In FAANG, the equivalent is a reverse proxy (nginx, Envoy, Kong) with network policies (Kubernetes NetworkPolicies, AWS Security Groups) in a zero trust architecture where no system is trusted by default. Interview framing: "SAProuter is an allow-list-based application firewall — it defines precisely which source-destination pairs are permitted. This is the same concept as Kubernetes NetworkPolicies or AWS security groups in a zero trust model."

class="s-sub" x="360" y="307">Network policies, mTLS grid</text></svg>

SAP's workflow and batch management systems are enterprise-grade process orchestration engines. The patterns translate directly to FAANG's orchestration systems.

SAP Workflow → BPMN + Orchestration Engine (Temporal / Cadence). SAP Business Workflow is a process orchestration engine: you define a workflow (approval chain, multi-step process), assign agents (roles, users, positions), set deadlines, and handle escalations. The workflow instance tracks state through each step, with step-by-step progress visible in SWIA. This is BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation) implemented in ABAP. In FAANG, the equivalent is Temporal (or Cadence, Uber's predecessor): durable execution of workflows with state persistence, retry, timeout, and escalation. Interview framing: "SAP Workflow is a BPMN orchestration engine with durable execution. Each workflow instance persists its state between steps, supports retry and timeout (deadlines), and escalates on failure. This is the same pattern as Temporal workflows — orchestrated multi-step processes with guaranteed execution."

SAP Batch Management → Job Scheduler (Apache Airflow / Dagster). SAP's batch job scheduling (SM36) manages the execution of background jobs with dependencies, periodic schedules, event-triggered starts, and job chains. This is a job scheduler / workflow orchestrator — the same function as Apache Airflow (DAG-based scheduling), Cron (time-based), or Kubernetes CronJobs. Interview framing: "I managed enterprise batch orchestration with 2,000+ scheduled jobs, dependency chains, and event-driven triggers. The principles are identical to Apache Airflow DAGs or Kubernetes CronJobs: define the schedule, manage dependencies, handle failures with retry logic."

engine</text><text class="w-sub" x="120" y="122">Durable execution, agents, deadlines</text><line x1="220" y1="97" x2="260" y2="97" class="w-edge"/><rect class="w-faang" x="260" y="65" width="200" height="65"/><text class="w-lbl" x="360" y="87">FAANG: Temporal / Cadence</text><text class="w-sub" x="360" y="105">Durable execution orchestration</text><text class="w-sub" x="360" y="122">Retry, timeout, state persistence</text><rect class="w-sap" x="20" y="165" width="200" height="65"/><text class="w-lbl" x="120" y="187">SAP: Batch (SM36)</text><text class="w-sub" x="120" y="205">2,000+ scheduled jobs with dependencies</text><text class="w-sub" x="120" y="222">Periodic schedules, event triggers</text><line x1="220" y1="197" x2="260" y2="197" class="w-edge"/><rect class="w-faang" x="260" y="165" width="200" height="65"/><text class="w-lbl" x="360" y="187">FAANG: Airflow / Dagster</text><text class="w-sub" x="360" y="205">DAG-based job orchestration</text><text class="w-sub" x="360" y="222">Dependency management, retries</text></svg>

These are 30-second answers you can use directly in interviews. Practice each one until it sounds natural, not rehearsed. The pattern is: Name the SAP concept → translate to DS equivalent → show depth with a specific detail → offer to go deeper.

When asked about your experience with messaging systems: "I worked with SAP's event-driven architecture for 10+ years. IDoc/ALE is change data capture — it detects business object changes, serializes them into typed documents, and distributes them to subscribers. The patterns I used (schema evolution, idempotent processing, exactly-once delivery) are identical to Kafka. I am comfortable with event sourcing, stream processing, and schema registries."

When asked about distributed transactions: "SAP LUW implements the Saga pattern. A purchase-to-pay process involves creating a purchase order, posting goods receipt, and processing invoice — each step with compensating actions on failure. The transaction manager coordinates commit or rollback across all steps. In FAANG terms, I designed saga choreographies with compensation handlers."

When asked about API design: "I designed BAPI interfaces — typed service contracts with input/output parameters, error handling, and versioning — for 50+ business objects. This is identical to designing gRPC services with Protobuf. I understand contract-first API design, backward compatibility, and deprecation strategies."

When asked about deployment: "SAP gCTS is GitOps for enterprise — Git as source of truth, pull-based deployment, declarative configurations. I understand the GitOps model and its limitations (no traffic splitting, coarse rollback). I am eager to work with canary deployments, feature flags, and progressive delivery."

When asked about security: "SAP authorization objects implement RBAC/ABAC with multi-field conditions. I designed access control for 50,000 users with role definitions, permission boundaries, and audit trails. The principles translate directly to AWS IAM or Azure RBAC."

When asked about the biggest difference between SAP and FAANG: "The engineering culture, not the concepts. The distributed systems principles are the same — SAP implements them in a proprietary runtime, FAANG implements them in cloud-native infrastructure. The algorithms (Saga, event sourcing, RBAC, BPMN, CDC) are identical. The difference is velocity: FAANG deploys 200 times per day with canary rollouts and instant rollback. SAP deploys twice per month with transports. I understand both worlds and am ready to operate at FAANG velocity."

distributed transactions?"</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="90">"SAP LUW is the Saga pattern — multi-step business</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="107">transactions with compensating Protobuf/gRPC — typed contracts,</text><text class="i-sub" x="180" y="197">versioning, backward compatibility."</text><rect class="i-card" x="365" y="145" width="310" height="70"/><text class="i-lbl" x="520" y="162">"Describe your deployment experience"</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="180">"SAP gCTS is GitOps — Git-driven pull-based</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="197">deployment, same as ArgoCD."</text><rect class="i-card" x="25" y="235" width="310" height="70"/><text class="i-lbl" x="180" y="252">"What about security and access control?"</text><text class="i-sub" x="180" y="270">"SAP auth objects = RBAC/ABAC. I designed for</text><text class="i-sub" x="180" y="287">50K users — directly maps to IAM."</text><rect class="i-card" x="365" y="235" width="310" height="70"/><text class="i-lbl" x="520" y="252">"SAP vs FAANG — biggest difference?"</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="270">"The concepts are identical. The difference is</text><text class="i-sub" x="520" y="287">velocity: 200 deploys/day vs 2/month."</text><text class="i-lbl" x="350" y="340" fill="var(--brand-primary)">Pattern: "SAP concept is DS pattern — I understand both implementations"</text></svg>

details: Staff+ Insight: The Translation Map is the single most powerful tool for an SAP engineer in FAANG interviews. Every concept you know in SAP has a name and a community in distributed systems. Your job is not to learn new concepts — it is to learn the new vocabulary. Spend 10 minutes per day for 10 days: learn 2-3 pairs per session. After 10 days, you will have vocabulary fluency for 20+ FAANG concepts. That is 10 days of work to overcome the #1 barrier for SAP engineers in FAANG interviews.
